text: The surgeon inserts the epigastric port to the right of the falciform ligament, ensuring it passes through the rectus muscle to minimize the risk of hernia formation.
The surgeon identifies omental adhesions in the surgical field during the laparoscopic cholecystectomy procedure.
The patient is positioned for a laparoscopic cholecystectomy, with the surgical team preparing to proceed with the procedure.
The surgeon carefully opens and splays the omentum, ensuring that only omental tissue is present and avoiding proximity to the colon or duodenum.
The laparoscopic view reveals the stomach and the duodenal portion, clearly visible within the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a monopolar cautery set at a low wattage of twenty watts to perform precise dissection, minimizing tissue charring and smoke generation. Energy is applied in short bursts to prevent excessive heat buildup.
The surgeon adjusts the view using a thirty-degree telescope, enhancing the visualization of the surgical field.
The surgeon closely adheres to the gallbladder, identifying and dividing thin fibrous strands to push the omentum away from the dissection area.
The surgeon exposes the gallbladder, which is covered by a significant amount of surrounding fat tissue, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon encounters difficulty in visually identifying the lower border of the gallbladder, which is obscured by surrounding tissue.
The video shows a thick layer of fat covering the gallbladder, making it visually challenging to identify the gallbladder's lower border.
The supraumbilical port placement provides a clear view of the surgical field, minimizing the visibility of the stomach and duodenum during the procedure.
The surgical field provides an unobstructed view of the gallbladder area, facilitating meticulous dissection despite the presence of thick visceral fat.
The surgeon gradually removes the thick layer of fat covering the gallbladder, exposing its borders and facilitating the identification of the cystic duct.
The surgeon performs a gradual dissection around the gallbladder to enhance exposure of the anatomical structures, preparing for ligation or clipping of the cystic duct and artery.
The surgeon performs a posterior dissection, removing the peritoneum and surrounding fat from the gallbladder, revealing its anatomical borders.
The surgeon proceeds with anterior dissection of the gallbladder, focusing on the area previously cleared of thick fat to better delineate the gallbladder's borders and approach the cystic duct.
The surgeon applies traction and counter traction to the gallbladder, enhancing the exposure of the surgical field for continued dissection.
The assistant holds the gallbladder towards the patient's right shoulder, providing traction to facilitate further dissection and exposure of the surrounding anatomical structures.

The surgeon provides counter traction with the assisting hand, holding the gallbladder at the infundibulum or Hartmann's pouch area. Gradual dissection is performed to open the anterior peritoneum, demonstrating precise technique facilitated by effective traction and counter traction.
The surgeon carefully dissects close to the gallbladder, ensuring precision to avoid injury to nearby vital structures.
The surgeon performs a precise dissection in small portions, minimizing bleeding and maintaining a clear surgical field for optimal visibility and sharp dissection.
The surgeon continues dissection on both the anterior and posterior sides of the gallbladder, with visible traction applied by the assistant holding the gallbladder towards the right shoulder.
The surgeon performs dissection around the gallbladder, ensuring a comprehensive approach by working on multiple areas rather than focusing on a single point.
The surgeon gradually opens the peritoneum, moving closer to the liver to fully expose the gallbladder, establishing a clear plane of dissection.
The entire gallbladder is visible in the surgical field, with its anatomical features clearly distinguishable.
The elongated gallbladder is clearly visible, allowing for detailed observation of its anatomical features.
The elongated gallbladder becomes increasingly visible, allowing for better identification of its length and anatomical features.
The surgeon maintains close proximity to the gallbladder during dissection, ensuring precise exposure and manipulation of the anatomical structure.
The surgeon applies electrocautery burns directly on the gallbladder, avoiding other nearby structures, while working close to important anatomical features.
The surgeon exposes the gallbladder, revealing a small portion of residual fat near the liver bed, highlighting the anatomical relationship between these structures.
The surgeon holds the elongated gallbladder with traction and counter-traction, gradually dissecting it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies and explores a clearly visualized dissection plane, proceeding into adjacent areas that appear more challenging.
The surgeon performs a combination of sharp and blunt dissection around the gallbladder, carefully navigating unclear tissue planes to avoid damaging vital structures.
The surgeon carefully progresses the dissection by separating small strands of tissue, ensuring precise and controlled advancement through the surgical field.
The surgeon applies traction using a hook, revealing a thin layer of fat beneath the tissue with no additional structures visible beyond it.

The surgeon carefully opens the anterior and posterior peritoneum to isolate the gallbladder. The cystic duct and cystic artery are dissected separately, followed by the dissection of the lower portion of the gallbladder away from the liver bed. This technique ensures a clear critical view with only two structures leading to the gallbladder.
The surgeon navigates dense omental adhesions, revealing small vessels indicative of neovascularization around the gallbladder.
The surgeon uses a bipolar device to cauterize tissue, applying a single clip proximally for added security. The bipolar is used distally, ensuring it is well away from the clip.
The surgeon carefully identifies and isolates individual strands of tissue, preparing to dissect and cut them using energy devices, ensuring precise separation of structures.
The surgeon places a single clip proximally on the structure and uses a bipolar cautery device well away from the clip to prevent thermal spread during the procedure.
The video shows a segment of normal tissue length between the area of coagulation and the clip, demonstrating careful placement to avoid thermal spread.

The surgeon maintains a safe distance from the clip while using energy devices, opting to place an additional clip distally and use cold scissors for cutting. A small vessel showing neovascularization is visible, commonly seen in prolonged cholecystitis cases.

The surgeon applies good traction to the gallbladder, ensuring clear identification of anterior and posterior structures before using energy devices. The gallbladder is visibly contracted and elongated.
The surgeon displays the dissected fat that has been separated from the gallbladder, highlighting the amount removed during the procedure.
The surgeon continues the dissection, revealing the elongated and contracted gallbladder, which is visibly long despite starting the dissection at a higher point.
The surgeon dissects the remaining fatty structures from the gallbladder, carefully removing them in an individualized manner.
The surgeon applies traction away from the gallbladder to open up the dissection plane, ensuring no vital structures are present before using cautery.
The surgeon performs a precise dissection, progressing millimeter by millimeter towards the area of interest near the cystic duct, gradually exposing the entire cystic duct.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the gallbladder, maintaining close proximity to its surface to ensure precise separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues meticulous dissection around the gallbladder, ensuring clear exposure of anatomical planes while avoiding vital structures.
The surgeon identifies the peritoneum over the gallbladder bed and carefully dissects between the liver and the peritoneum to avoid causing charring in the liver bed.
The surgeon continues to dissect the peritoneum over the gallbladder bed, carefully separating it from the liver tissue.
A large abnormal vessel is visible running close to the gallbladder, but it does not obstruct the dissection path.
The surgeon observes the gallbladder narrowing and becoming elongated, indicating proximity to the lower end or cystic duct. An abnormal vessel is visible but remains outside the dissection area.

The surgeon performs a retrograde dissection of the gallbladder, initiating a fundus-first approach. At the mid-level of the gallbladder, attachments to the liver bed are opened, creating a visible window through which the liver can be seen, while leaving some attachments intact.

The surgeon releases the last attachments of the gallbladder to the liver near the fundus, preventing the gallbladder from hanging freely. The dissection then moves lower to isolate the cystic duct, ensuring stable manipulation of the gallbladder.
The surgeon performs a sharp dissection after isolating the tissues, ensuring no structures are present behind the targeted area before using energy devices to cut the tissue.
Small vessels are visible in the surgical field, and the surgeon coagulates them using monopolar energy.
The surgeon carefully removes the adipose tissue covering the cystic duct, revealing the duct's structure for further dissection.
The surgeon isolates and dissects the cystic duct, ensuring it is separated from surrounding tissues to facilitate secure placement of clips, minimizing the risk of retraction and potential leaks.
The surgeon addresses the fat covering the cystic duct, preparing to remove it for better visualization and access to the duct.
The surgeon holds the cystic duct and continues dissection to improve clarity, removing fat from the area. The plane between the cystic duct and the artery is gradually being opened up.
The surgeon uses a hook dissector for precise dissection around the cystic duct, avoiding the tendency to inadvertently grasp or strip structures, which can occur with a Maryland dissector.
The surgeon completes the dissection, revealing an open window between the cystic duct and artery, with clear visibility of the anatomical structures.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to open the tissue plane in a semi-blunt manner, simultaneously dissecting anteriorly and posteriorly. The posterior view is assessed to ensure the adequacy of the dissection.
The surgeon removes a small amount of fat tissue to ensure complete isolation of the cystic duct, preparing for further dissection.
The surgeon gradually exposes the cystic artery during the dissection, revealing its position relative to the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon prepares to isolate and dissect the visible anatomical structures, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully dissects the adipose tissue located near the cystic artery, using precise techniques to ensure clear visualization and isolation of the artery.
The surgeon removes connective tissue strands surrounding the cystic artery and duct, clearing the area for further dissection.
The surgeon continues to dissect and isolate the vessel, progressively revealing more of the cystic artery as surrounding tissue is carefully removed.
The surgeon gradually exposes the critical view of safety, revealing the cystic duct and artery clearly separated from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety during the cholecystectomy, with clear visualization of the cystic duct and artery, ensuring these structures are isolated from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon holds the proximal cystic duct while identifying the adjacent cystic artery, both structures clearly visible in the surgical field.
Beyond the cystic artery, an open space is visible, allowing a clear view of the liver.
The surgeon retracts the liver further, enhancing the exposure of the critical view, which includes the cystic duct and artery, allowing for better visualization of these structures.
The gallbladder is retracted away, revealing the cystic duct and cystic artery as the only two visible structures extending from the gallbladder.
The surgeon confirms the posterior view of the surgical field, with the gallbladder retracted and the cystic duct and artery visible.
The surgeon retracts the liver, revealing two structures extending from the gallbladder, with the liver clearly visible in the background.
The surgeon has dissected up to the junction of the cystic duct and the common bile duct (CBD), with the gallbladder visibly separated from surrounding structures.
The surgeon presents the critical view of safety, clearly displaying the cystic duct and artery with the gallbladder separated from the liver, ensuring no risk to the common bile duct.
The surgeon observes the widening portion of the common bile duct (CBD), visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon applies clips to the cystic artery, securing it before proceeding with further dissection.
The surgeon reduces traction on the gallbladder, allowing for a clearer view of the dissected area near the junction of the cystic duct and common bile duct (CBD).
The surgeon observes the cystic artery, noting that it is not completely stretched out, indicating careful manipulation and positioning within the surgical field.
The surgeon applies two proximal clips and one distal clip to secure the cystic duct or artery, ensuring proper ligation within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs milking of the cystic duct from the junction of the common bile duct (CBD) to ensure no stones are present in this section.

The surgeon leaves traction on the cystic duct, showing its relaxed state. A clip is applied at least a centimeter away from the cystic duct-CBD junction. When traction is applied, the cystic duct appears elongated, and the junction may be pulled up, which requires careful attention to avoid errors.
The surgical field shows a well-dissected area around the cystic duct, providing ample space for further manipulation and visualization of the anatomical structures.
The surgeon divides the cystic artery before addressing the cystic duct to prevent the artery from snapping due to traction.
The surgeon cuts the cystic duct, leaving a small cuff of tissue beyond the applied clip to prevent slippage. The clip is securely placed to ensure stability during the cutting process.
The surgeon begins dissecting a small portion of the remnant gallbladder from the liver bed, carefully separating it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon pulls the gallbladder away from the liver, exposing an avascular plane for dissection.
The surgeon carefully dissects through the thick layer of fat surrounding the gallbladder, avoiding deep injury to the liver parenchyma to prevent excessive coagulation and charred tissue formation.
The surgeon meticulously divides the visible strands of tissue connecting the gallbladder to the liver bed, ensuring careful separation.

The surgeon carefully dissects the remaining strands adhering the gallbladder to the liver bed, ensuring good hemostasis and control. The dissection is performed in the identified avascular plane, with attention to avoiding injury to the gallbladder despite the presence of residual fat.
The surgeon identifies and continues dissection through a clear avascular plane, carefully avoiding injury to the liver while separating the gallbladder from the liver bed.
The surgeon carefully dissects the last few attachments keeping the gallbladder connected to the liver bed, ensuring precise separation without injuring surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues to dissect through the thick layer of fat surrounding the gallbladder, which is visibly dense and requires careful, prolonged dissection.
The surgeon uses a hook electrocautery device, with visible charring on the tissue, which may reduce the efficiency of current transmission during dissection.
The surgeon examines the gallbladder bed, checking for any signs of active bleeding or bile leakage from an accessory duct.
The surgeon places the gallbladder into an endobag, a simple bag adapted from the suction apparatus, to facilitate its removal through a minimal incision.

The surgeon makes an incision at the neck of the gallbladder to suck out bile and remove large stones if present. The cystic duct is clipped and positioned towards the edge of the endobag, allowing for controlled manipulation and gradual dissection of the gallbladder neck or duct.
The surgeon uses suction to gradually pull the gallbladder, leaving the endobag intact, while ensuring all actions are performed under direct vision. The gallbladder is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon closes the port using transfacial sutures, ensuring security, and proceeds to close the umbilical port.
